faq.label: Frequently Asked
faq.title: Frequently asked questions.
faq.intro: We've answered the most common questions. Something else? Contact us.
faq.q1: Do I need a medical referral?
faq.a1: It's not required. If you have imaging (MRI, ultrasound) or a medical referral, please bring it — it helps us personalise your treatment plan from the start.
faq.q2: How many sessions are usually needed?
faq.a2: It depends on the condition. Acute injuries can resolve in 4–8 sessions; chronic conditions require longer follow-up. At the first consultation we always set a realistic and honest plan.
faq.q3: Is Clinical Pilates different from regular Pilates?
faq.a3: Yes, significantly. It's supervised by a healthcare professional, with exercises prescribed specifically for your condition — it's not a generic group class. It's rehabilitation and prevention.
faq.a4: Most patients describe just a gentle pressure sensation. We use very fine needles with patient comfort as a priority. Some mild local tenderness over the next 24–48h is normal.
faq.a5: Yes, there's free parking near Rua Nova de Agrafonte. If you have difficulty, give us a call and we'll guide you straight away.
faq.q6: Do you treat children and babies?
faq.a6: Yes. Paediatric osteopathy lets us treat newborns, babies and children through adolescence. The techniques are fully adapted and gentle — parents are always welcome in the room.

sobre.hero.sub: Discover Fisio Vitae — the clinic founded with passion by Dr. Cláudia Alves, which over 20 years has become a reference in physiotherapy and osteopathy in the Braga region.
sobre.hist.photo_quote: "Treatment begins by listening. Every body tells a different story."
sobre.hist.label: Our Origins
sobre.hist.title: A clinic with soul
sobre.hist.p1: Fisio Vitae was born from the determination and passion of Dr. Cláudia Alves, a physiotherapist with a clear vision: to create a space where clinical excellence and human warmth coexisted in perfect harmony.
sobre.hist.p2: Founded in Panoias, Braga, the clinic began as an intimate project, with one certainty: that real treatment begins by listening. Listening to the pain, listening to the story, listening to the person — not just the diagnosis.
sobre.hist.p3: Over two decades, Fisio Vitae grew organically, always guided by the same principles: scientific rigour, continuous learning and a genuinely welcoming environment. Every new team member was carefully chosen to share these values.
sobre.hist.p4: Today we are a multidisciplinary team combining cutting-edge physiotherapy — including percutaneous and ultrasound-guided techniques — with clinical pilates and osteopathy, bringing to Braga patients the level of care previously only found in major cities.
sobre.jornada.label: Our Journey
sobre.jornada.title: A story of evolution
sobre.jornada.sub: The milestones that shaped Fisio Vitae over the years.
sobre.jornada.2004.title: Foundation of Fisio Vitae
sobre.jornada.2004.text: Dr. Cláudia Alves opens the clinic in Panoias, Braga, with the mission of bringing quality physiotherapy to the local community, with a patient-centred approach.
sobre.jornada.2008.title: Team Expansion
sobre.jornada.2008.text: Inês Macedo joins the team as a physiotherapist, bringing new expertise in osteopathy and musculoskeletal rehabilitation. The clinic doubles its capacity.
sobre.jornada.2013.title: Introduction of Clinical Pilates
sobre.jornada.2013.text: José Pereira joins the team as a Clinical Pilates specialist, making Fisio Vitae one of the first clinics in the region to integrate therapeutic pilates into rehabilitation.
sobre.jornada.2017.title: Percutaneous Physiotherapy
sobre.jornada.2017.text: Dr. Cláudia obtains advanced certification in percutaneous physiotherapy, making Fisio Vitae a regional reference in this minimally invasive pain treatment technique.
sobre.jornada.2020.title: Ultrasound-Guided Physiotherapy
sobre.jornada.2020.text: Investment in state-of-the-art ultrasound equipment and advanced training in ultrasound-guided physiotherapy. The clinic begins offering treatments with real-time imaging precision.
sobre.jornada.2024.title: 20 Years of Excellence
sobre.jornada.2024.text: Fisio Vitae celebrates two decades of activity. Hundreds of patients recovered, a united team and a renewed commitment to remain the reference clinic in Braga.
sobre.missao.label: What Drives Us
sobre.missao.title: Mission & Values
sobre.missao.p1: Restoring movement, health and quality of life to every patient, through specialised, personalised care grounded in the most current scientific evidence.
sobre.missao.p2: We believe physiotherapy is not just symptom treatment — it is a collaborative process between therapist and patient, where education, empowerment and close follow-up make all the difference.
sobre.missao.p3: Our commitment is that everyone who enters Fisio Vitae leaves with a clear plan, renewed hope and, above all, real and lasting results.

contacto.faq.label: Questions
contacto.faq.title: Questions about appointments
contacto.faq.q1: How long does the first appointment take?
contacto.faq.a1: The first appointment includes a full assessment and usually takes 45 to 60 minutes. Follow-up sessions are normally 30 to 45 minutes, depending on the service. Clinical Pilates sessions are 50–60 minutes.
contacto.faq.q2: Can I cancel or reschedule my appointment?
contacto.faq.a2: Yes. We ask that any cancellation or reschedule be done at least 24 hours in advance, so we can offer the slot to other patients. You can cancel by phone, email or WhatsApp.
contacto.faq.q4: What should I bring to the first appointment?
contacto.faq.a4: We recommend bringing: ID card, health card, and any relevant diagnostic exams (MRI, ultrasound, blood tests). If you have a medical report or referral, bring those too. Wear comfortable clothing that allows access to the area to be treated.
contacto.faq.q5: How long is the waiting time for an appointment?
contacto.faq.a5: We can usually offer an appointment within 2–5 working days. During busier periods it may be slightly longer, but we always contact you as soon as availability opens up. For urgent cases, we recommend calling directly.

percutanea.hero.title: Percutaneous Physiotherapy
percutanea.hero.sub: Minimally invasive treatment for chronic pain, tendinopathies and myofascial trigger points. Fine needles guided with anatomical precision — results where other methods fall short.
percutanea.about.label: The Treatment
percutanea.about.title: What is Percutaneous Physiotherapy?
percutanea.about.p1: Percutaneous physiotherapy — also known as dry needling — involves inserting fine acupuncture needles directly into target tissues. Unlike traditional acupuncture, it has a fully scientific and anatomical basis, acting on myofascial trigger points, tendons and connective tissue.
percutanea.about.p2: The technique triggers a local neuromodulatory response that breaks chronic pain cycles, reduces muscle spasm and stimulates the natural tissue regeneration mechanisms.
percutanea.steps.1.title: Assessment:
percutanea.steps.1.text: Identification of target tissues and pain points through palpation and functional testing.
percutanea.steps.2.title: Application:
percutanea.steps.2.text: Insertion of fine needles at the identified points with anatomical precision.
percutanea.steps.3.title: Stimulation:
percutanea.steps.3.text: Gentle movements to trigger the desired local twitch response (LTR).
percutanea.steps.4.title: Recovery:
percutanea.steps.4.text: Post-treatment mobilisation and guidance for the following 24–48h.
percutanea.ind.1: Chronic neck and back pain — cervicalgia, lumbago and referred pain
percutanea.ind.5: Myofascial trigger points — referred muscle pain and range of motion limitation
percutanea.quickinfo.title: Percutaneous Physiotherapy
ecoguiada.hero.title: Ultrasound-Guided Physiotherapy
ecoguiada.hero.sub: Treatments guided by real-time ultrasound — maximum precision, minimum risk. The most advanced technique in interventional physiotherapy.
ecoguiada.about.label: The Treatment
ecoguiada.about.title: What is Ultrasound-Guided Physiotherapy?
ecoguiada.about.p1: Ultrasound-guided physiotherapy represents the state of the art in treating musculoskeletal injuries — guided by real-time ultrasound, it allows a surgical precision that other techniques cannot match.
ecoguiada.about.p2: Using a high-resolution ultrasound scanner, the therapist can visualise internal structures in real time — tendons, muscles, synovial bursae, nerves — and guide needles or other instruments with millimetric precision.
ecoguiada.steps.1.text: In-office diagnosis: Ultrasound assesses the condition of tissues without the need for external exams.
ecoguiada.steps.2.text: Maximum precision: Real-time visualisation avoids adjacent vascular and nerve structures.
ecoguiada.steps.3.text: Proven effectiveness: Superior results in chronic tendon injuries and bursitis.
ecoguiada.steps.4.text: Documentation: Before-and-after treatment images for objective tracking of progress.
ecoguiada.ind.1: Chronic tendinopathies — hard to resolve with conventional methods
ecoguiada.ind.2: Bursitis — inflammation of the synovial bursae of the shoulder, hip and knee

team.hero.desc: At Fisio Vitae we bring together physiotherapists, an osteopath, a nutritionist and instructors with a shared mission: to restore your movement, well-being and quality of life — with technical rigour and genuine attention to each case.

team.strip.title:
Five professionals,
one shared vision
team.claudia.mc_sub: Physiotherapist · Osteopath · Invasive Physiotherapy · 20+ Years of Experience
team.claudia.bio1: Dr. Cláudia Alves is the founder and clinical director of Fisio Vitae. With over two decades dedicated to physiotherapy, she has built one of the leading clinics in the Braga region — through technical excellence and the philosophy she has always upheld: listen before treating.
team.claudia.bio2: She holds advanced training in Osteopathy and completed a Postgraduate degree in Invasive Physiotherapy, integrating ultrasound-guided and percutaneous techniques into her daily clinical practice.
team.claudia.quote: "I made my clinic into the place I would want to find as a patient myself: somewhere I feel heard, where treatment is explained, and where the goal is always to give me back the life I had before the injury."
team.ines.bio1: Inês Macedo is a physiotherapist at Fisio Vitae, committed to a patient-centred approach focused on lasting functional results.
team.ines.bio2: Her work combines current manual and therapeutic techniques, always with a focus on restoring movement and preventing recurring injuries.
team.patricia.bio1: Patrícia joins the Fisio Vitae team as a nutritionist, bringing a holistic view to patient recovery. She believes that nutrition and rehabilitation go hand in hand for a more complete result.
team.patricia.bio2: She supports patients through weight loss, chronic disease management, sports nutrition and individualised meal plans tailored to each person's goals.
team.jose.bio1: José Pereira is a unique presence at Fisio Vitae, combining two complementary fields: Clinical Pilates as a tool for rehabilitation and strengthening, and Reiki Analysis as support for patients' energetic and emotional wellbeing.
team.jose.bio2: He develops adapted pilates programmes in close coordination with the clinical team, ensuring every exercise serves each person's specific recovery.
team.rafaela.bio1: Rafaela Torres is the first face of Fisio Vitae — the voice that answers the call, the smile that greets you at the door, the person who makes sure every appointment and every detail is handled with care and attention.
team.rafaela.bio2: She coordinates the clinic's schedule and ensures that every patient's experience, from the first contact to the end of treatment, is smooth and welcoming.

drenagem.about.p1: An extremely gentle manual technique that stimulates the circulation of the lymphatic system, helping the body eliminate retained fluids and toxins and reduce swelling.
drenagem.about.p2: Manual lymphatic drainage is based on the Vodder method, using slow, rhythmic circular movements with very light pressure. The strokes follow the natural path of the lymphatic vessels, toward the lymph nodes.
drenagem.about.p3: It is suitable for a range of conditions — from post-surgery recovery (plastic surgery, mastectomy) to the treatment of heavy legs, sinusitis or fluid retention during pregnancy.
drenagem.steps.1.text: Assessment: Analysis of the lymphatic state and areas of swelling.
drenagem.steps.2.text: Node clearing: Opening of the key points (cervical, axillary, inguinal).
drenagem.steps.3.text: Drainage: Gentle, rhythmic strokes toward the lymph nodes.
drenagem.ind.1: Swelling and fluid retention — heavy, swollen legs
electro.about.p1: A combination of traditional acupuncture with controlled electrical microcurrents, enhancing the treatment's analgesic, anti-inflammatory and neuromuscular effects.
electro.about.p2: Electroacupuncture applies a low-frequency electrical current through the acupuncture needles, intensifying point stimulation without increasing discomfort. The electrical parameters (frequency, intensity) are adjusted according to the therapeutic goal.
electro.about.p3: It is particularly effective in treating chronic pain, recovering from neurological injuries and controlling muscle spasms, with solid scientific backing from clinical studies.
electro.steps.1.text: Assessment: Identification of the key points and the appropriate settings.
electro.steps.2.text: Application: Insertion of the fine needles at the selected points.
electro.steps.3.text: Stimulation: Connection of the needles to the device with adjusted frequency.
electro.steps.4.text: Rest: A 20–30 minute session with the stimulation active.
electro.ind.1: Chronic pain — low back pain, neck pain and neuropathic pain
electro.ind.5: Stress and anxiety — regulation of the nervous system
estetica.about.p1: Personalised facial and body aesthetic treatments, combining professional techniques with high-quality products to enhance your natural beauty.
estetica.about.p2: Our aesthetics space offers a wide range of treatments tailored to each skin type and need — deep cleansing facials, peels, hydration, anti-ageing treatments, waxing, manicures and pedicures.
estetica.about.p3: Every protocol begins with a careful analysis of your skin, your needs and your personal goals, so that the chosen treatment delivers visible, lasting results.
estetica.steps.1.text: Analysis: Skin assessment and identification of needs.
estetica.steps.2.text: Preparation: Cleansing and preparation of the area to be treated.
estetica.steps.4.text: Maintenance: Daily care advice and recommended products.
estetica.ind.1: Facial care — cleansing facials, hydration and targeted treatments
estetica.ind.4: Waxing — hot wax, warm wax and intimate areas
estetica.ind.5: Manicure and Pedicure — complete hand and foot care

anticelulite.about.p1: An intensive manual treatment targeting areas of fat accumulation and cellulite, combining specific techniques that act on the fibrous nodules and improve local circulation.
anticelulite.about.p2: Anti-cellulite massage uses deep kneading, rolling and gliding-pressure techniques to mobilise the fatty tissue, break up fibrous adhesions and stimulate the drainage of retained fluids.
anticelulite.about.p3: For visible results, a protocol of several sessions is recommended, combined with adequate hydration and regular physical activity. The effects include smoother skin, reduced volume and improved muscle tone.
anticelulite.steps.1.text: Assessment: Analysis of the areas to treat and the type of cellulite.
anticelulite.steps.2.text: Warm-up: Initial strokes to prepare the tissues.
anticelulite.steps.3.text: Treatment: Intensive kneading and deep mobilisation techniques.
anticelulite.steps.4.text: Finishing: Upward drainage and application of a firming product.
anticelulite.ind.1: Cellulite — grades 1 to 3, on thighs, buttocks and abdomen
anticelulite.ind.2: Fluid retention — localised swelling and a feeling of heaviness
anticelulite.ind.3: Tissue laxity — loss of firmness and skin tone
anticelulite.ind.4: Localised fat accumulation — areas resistant to diet and exercise
relax.about.p1: A complete sensory experience that combines gentle strokes, a steady rhythm and a soothing environment to quiet the mind and release the body from accumulated tension.
relax.about.p2: Relaxation massage uses long, flowing, continuous strokes across the whole body, with pressure adapted to each person's sensitivity. The goal is not to treat a specific injury, but to induce a deep state of neurovegetative relaxation.
relax.about.p3: The benefits extend to sleep quality, blood pressure, digestion and mood — an essential pause in a fast-paced daily life.
relax.steps.1.text: Welcome: A short conversation to understand your expectations and areas of tension.
relax.steps.2.text: Preparation: Warming the tissues with warm oil and superficial strokes.
relax.steps.3.text: Massage: Long, rhythmic strokes with light to medium pressure.
relax.steps.4.text: Closing: A gentle final touch and time to rest on the table.
relax.ind.1: Stress and fatigue — accumulated physical and mental tiredness
relax.ind.2: Generalised muscle tension — stiffness in the neck, shoulders and lower back
relax.ind.3: Insomnia and light sleep — difficulty falling asleep or resting
relax.ind.4: Mild anxiety — a constant feeling of tension
relax.ind.5: General well-being — self-care and a moment for yourself
meso.about.p1: A technique that combines classic mesotherapy with homeopathic active ingredients, delivered through superficial microinjections for localised treatment and revitalisation.
meso.about.p2: Homeopathic mesotherapy involves applying small amounts of homeopathic medicines directly into the dermis through minimally invasive microinjections. The active ingredients act locally, with minimal systemic effects.
meso.about.p3: It can be used for different goals — pain relief, anti-inflammatory action, cellulite reduction or facial revitalisation — combining the homeopathic ingredients best suited to each case.
meso.steps.1.text: Assessment: Defining the goal and selecting the homeopathic ingredients.
meso.steps.2.text: Preparation: Cleansing and disinfection of the area to be treated.
meso.steps.3.text: Application: Superficial microinjections with a very fine needle.
meso.steps.4.text: Aftercare: Post-treatment guidance and scheduling of the next session.
meso.ind.1: Musculoskeletal pain — muscle knots, tendinopathies, chronic pain
meso.ind.2: Cellulite and localised fat — body aesthetic protocol
estrutural.about.p1: Structural osteopathy starts from a fundamental premise: the human body is a single unit. When one structure becomes dysfunctional, others are inevitably affected. The osteopath treats the system, not just the symptom.
estrutural.about.p2: Through sophisticated manual techniques — joint manipulations, mobilisations, soft-tissue techniques and muscle energy techniques — the osteopath restores joint mobility and muscular balance, releasing the tensions at the root of the pain.
estrutural.about.p3: Osteopathy's holistic approach considers the interaction between the musculoskeletal, visceral and craniosacral systems, making it possible to treat conditions that seem unrelated to the muscles but actually stem from structural dysfunctions.
estrutural.how.title: Techniques used at Fisio Vitae
estrutural.steps.1.text: Joint manipulation (HVLA): High-velocity, low-amplitude techniques to restore joint mobility.
estrutural.steps.2.text: Soft-tissue techniques: Deep massage, inhibition and stretching for muscular and fascial release.
estrutural.steps.3.text: Muscle energy (MET): Active isometric contractions to correct joint dysfunctions.
estrutural.steps.4.text: Functional techniques: Gentle mobilisations that follow the natural movement of the tissue.
estrutural.ind.title: Conditions treated
estrutural.ind.1: Disc herniations and sciatica — relief of nerve-root compression
estrutural.ind.2: Osteoarthritis and joint stiffness — improved mobility and reduced pain
estrutural.ind.3: TMJ dysfunctions — bruxism, jaw and facial pain
estrutural.ind.4: Tension headaches and migraines — of musculoskeletal origin
estrutural.cta.question: Treat the cause, not just the symptom
ginecologica.about.p1: Osteopathic care dedicated to women's health at every stage of life — from pre-conception to postpartum, including the treatment of pelvic-floor dysfunctions and gynaecological discomfort.
ginecologica.about.p2: Gynaecological and pregnancy osteopathy uses gentle manual techniques to treat tensions and dysfunctions of the pelvis, sacrum, diaphragm and visceral structures that influence the female reproductive system. It is an approach complementary to conventional medicine, focused on restoring tissue mobility and postural balance.
ginecologica.about.p3: During pregnancy, it helps relieve low back pain, sciatica, reflux and pelvic discomfort caused by the body's adaptations. After birth, it supports the recovery of lumbopelvic alignment and the pelvic floor.
ginecologica.steps.1.text: History-taking: Detailed review of the gynaecological and obstetric history and current symptoms.
ginecologica.steps.2.text: Assessment: Postural analysis and palpation of the pelvic, abdominal and diaphragmatic structures.
ginecologica.steps.3.text: Treatment: Gentle manual techniques tailored to your current stage (pregnancy, postpartum, etc.).
ginecologica.steps.4.text: Guidance: Postural advice, exercises and care to maintain between sessions.
ginecologica.ind.1: Pregnancy — low back pain, sciatica, pelvic pain and pregnancy reflux
ginecologica.ind.2: Postpartum — lumbopelvic recovery, abdominal diastasis and scarring
ginecologica.ind.4: Fertility support — preparing the body for conception
ginecologica.ind.5: Menopause — managing physical symptoms and pelvic discomfort
pediatrica.about.p1: Paediatric osteopathy is a specialty that uses gentle, non-invasive techniques to treat babies, children and teenagers. It is one of our most sought-after and delicate services.
pediatrica.about.p2: Birth, even when it goes well, is a physically intense process for the baby. The forces involved during delivery can create tension in the newborn's cranial tissues and musculoskeletal system. Paediatric osteopathy intervenes early to release those tensions before they become dysfunctional patterns.
pediatrica.about.p3: All techniques used on babies and children are extremely gentle — often compared to the pressure of a sheet of paper — and completely safe when performed by a qualified professional.
pediatrica.how.title: Which ages is it suitable for?
pediatrica.steps.1.text: Newborns: Colic, breastfeeding difficulties, congenital torticollis, plagiocephaly.
pediatrica.steps.2.text: Babies (3–12m): Irritability, restless sleep, postural asymmetries, delayed motor development.
pediatrica.steps.3.text: Children (1–10 years): Postural problems, recurrent ear infections, respiratory issues, hyperactivity.
pediatrica.steps.4.text: Teenagers: Scoliosis, growing pains, sports injuries, headaches.
pediatrica.ind.title: Most common conditions
pediatrica.ind.1: Infant colic — irritability, intense crying, difficulty settling
pediatrica.ind.2: Congenital torticollis — persistent tilting of the head to one side
pediatrica.ind.4: Childhood and juvenile scoliosis — prevention and early treatment
pediatrica.ind.5: Recurrent ear infections — linked to cranial tension and lymphatic drainage
pediatrica.cta.question: Your child deserves the best care
pediatrica.cta.sub: Inês Macedo welcomes babies and children with all the gentleness and experience they need.
sacro.about.p1: An extremely gentle osteopathic approach that works with the natural rhythm of the craniosacral system, restoring the balance of the central nervous system and releasing deep tensions.
sacro.about.p2: Sacrocranial osteopathy is based on perceiving and adjusting the rhythmic movement of the cerebrospinal fluid between the skull and the sacrum. Using very light touches (around 5 grams of pressure), the therapist detects restrictions in the membranes and tissues surrounding the central nervous system.
sacro.about.p3: It is suitable for all ages — from newborns to the elderly — and is particularly effective in treating headaches, stress, anxiety, sleep disorders and the after-effects of trauma.
sacro.steps.1.text: Assessment: Careful listening to the craniosacral rhythm and identification of membranous restrictions.
sacro.steps.2.text: Gentle touch: Application of very light pressure to the skull, spine and sacrum.
sacro.steps.3.text: Release: Following the fascial releases and rebalancing of the tissues.
sacro.steps.4.text: Integration: A rest period for the nervous system to integrate the changes.
sacro.ind.1: Headaches and migraines — recurrent headaches and cranial tension
sacro.ind.2: Stress and anxiety — regulation of the autonomic nervous system
sacro.ind.4: After-effects of trauma — whiplash, falls, cranial surgery
visceral.about.p1: An osteopathic approach focused on the mobility and motility of the internal organs — liver, stomach, intestines, kidneys — restoring visceral function and relieving abdominal tension.
visceral.about.p2: The internal organs have their own movement and gliding relationships with neighbouring structures. When they lose mobility — through surgery, inflammation, stress or posture — they create restrictions that show up as abdominal pain, digestive problems or even low back and neck pain.
visceral.about.p3: Visceral osteopathy uses specific manual techniques to restore organ mobility, improve local circulation and support the function of the digestive, urinary and reproductive systems.
visceral.steps.1.text: Assessment: Abdominal palpation and visceral mobility tests.
visceral.steps.2.text: Mobilisation: Gentle manual techniques on the restricted organs.
visceral.steps.3.text: Release: Work on the fascial and ligamentous adhesions.
visceral.steps.4.text: Rebalancing: Integration with overall posture and dietary guidance.
visceral.ind.1: Gastroesophageal reflux — acidity, heartburn and difficult digestion
visceral.ind.2: Constipation and irritable bowel — slow or irregular bowel transit
visceral.ind.3: Chronic abdominal pain — with no identified organic cause
visceral.ind.5: Low back pain of visceral origin — referred pain from internal organs
pilates.about.p1: Clinical Pilates is far more than exercise — it is a powerful therapeutic tool that, when prescribed and supervised by a health professional, speeds up recovery and prevents relapses.
pilates.about.p2: At Fisio Vitae, Clinical Pilates is fully integrated into the treatment plan. José Pereira works closely with the physiotherapists to ensure that every exercise serves a specific clinical purpose, respecting each patient's limitations.
pilates.about.p3: We use professional equipment — reformer, Wunda chair and other apparatus — to offer a progressive programme that evolves with the patient, from the early stages of rehabilitation to a full return to activity.
pilates.how.title: How is the programme structured?
pilates.steps.1.text: Initial Assessment: Analysis of posture, strength, flexibility and the patient's specific limitations.
pilates.steps.2.text: Phase 1 — Awareness: Activation of the deep core, breathing and basic neuromuscular control.
pilates.steps.3.text: Phase 2 — Strengthening: Gradual progression of exercises on the reformer and chair, with increasing resistance.
pilates.steps.4.text: Phase 3 — Functionality: Integration of movement patterns into everyday activities and sport.
pilates.ind.title: Who is it for?
pilates.ind.2: Chronic pain — low back pain, neck pain, fibromyalgia
pilates.ind.4: Pregnancy and postpartum — strengthening of the pelvic floor
pilates.ind.5: Prevention — athletes and active people who want to reduce injury risk
pilates.cta.question: Start your Clinical Pilates programme
pilates.cta.sub: One-on-one or small-group sessions, always supervised.
reiki.about.p1: An energy therapy of Japanese origin that promotes physical, emotional and mental balance through the gentle laying-on of hands, channelling vital energy.
reiki.about.p2: Reiki — a Japanese word meaning universal life energy — is a non-invasive technique in which the therapist places their hands on or just above the body at specific points. It does not involve physical manipulation, pressure or massage.
reiki.about.p3: It works as a complementary therapy, helping to release energy blockages, reduce anxiety, promote deep relaxation and support healing processes. It is suitable for all ages and conditions.
reiki.steps.1.text: Welcome: A short conversation to understand your physical and emotional state.
reiki.steps.2.text: Preparation: Lying down comfortably on the table, clothed and relaxed.
reiki.steps.3.text: Laying-on of hands: Gentle application at specific energy points.
reiki.steps.4.text: Integration: Time to rest and share sensations.
reiki.ind.1: Stress and anxiety — a continuous feeling of tension and worry
reiki.ind.2: Emotional blockages — grief, separation or transition processes
reiki.ind.3: Chronic fatigue — persistent physical and mental tiredness
reiki.ind.5: Support during medical treatment — a complement to conventional therapies
rpg.about.p1: RPG is a therapeutic method developed by French physiotherapist Philippe Souchard that treats the body as a system of interconnected muscle chains — because posture is always global, never localised.
rpg.about.p2: Instead of treating only the painful area, RPG works on the posterior and anterior muscle chains as a whole. Through progressively held therapeutic postures, the method lengthens shortened muscles and corrects the postural patterns at the root of the problem.
rpg.about.p3: RPG sessions typically last 50–60 minutes, with the patient held in specific postures for several minutes while the therapist guides and adjusts. The method requires active participation and body awareness from the patient.
rpg.how.title: The core principles of RPG
rpg.steps.1.text: Globality: Each muscle is part of a chain. Treat the system, not the isolated muscle.
rpg.steps.2.text: Causality: The symptom is rarely where the cause is. RPG seeks the true origin of the problem.
rpg.steps.3.text: Individuality: Each person has their own specific posture. There are no protocols — there are people.
rpg.steps.4.text: Progression: The postures evolve as the patient gains awareness and flexibility.
rpg.ind.1: Scoliosis — prevention and correction of lateral curvatures of the spine
rpg.ind.2: Disc herniations — pressure relief and prevention of recurrence
rpg.ind.3: Chronic pain — persistent low back, neck and mid-back pain
rpg.ind.4: Flat and valgus feet — foundational postural correction through the lower muscle chain
rpg.ind.5: Postural deviations — dorsal hyperkyphosis, lumbar hyperlordosis, forward head
rpg.cta.question: Correct your posture. Eliminate the pain
rpg.cta.sub: One-on-one RPG sessions with Dr. Cláudia Alves.

blog.55: Percutaneous Physiotherapy: what it is, how it works and who it's for
blog.56: Percutaneous physiotherapy has been gaining increasing prominence in specialised clinics worldwide. In this article, Dr. Cláudia Alves explains in detail this minimally invasive technique that has been transforming the treatment of chronic pain and musculoskeletal injuries.
blog.58: Percutaneous physiotherapy — also known as dry needling — is a therapeutic technique that consists of inserting fine acupuncture needles through the skin, directly into muscle, tendon or fascial tissue, with the aim of treating pain and restoring function.
blog.59: Unlike traditional acupuncture, which is based on meridians and the principles of traditional Chinese medicine, percutaneous physiotherapy has an anatomical and neurophysiological foundation. The needles are directed at specific structures identified through rigorous clinical assessment.
blog.60: How does the mechanism of action work?
blog.61: The therapeutic effect of percutaneous physiotherapy occurs through several mechanisms:
blog.62: Local twitch response (LTR): The needle provokes a reflex contraction of the muscle that "deactivates" the trigger point, breaking the tension-pain-tension cycle.
blog.63: Neuromodulation: Mechanical stimulation activates nerve fibres that suppress pain transmission at the spinal and cortical level.
blog.64: Regulation of the biochemical environment: It alters the concentration of inflammatory and nociceptive substances in the treated tissue.
blog.65: Stimulation of regeneration: It causes controlled micro-trauma that triggers the tissue repair response.
blog.66: Which conditions is it indicated for?
blog.67: Scientific evidence supports the use of percutaneous physiotherapy across a wide range of conditions:
blog.68: Myofascial syndrome with trigger points — the most classic and most studied indication
blog.69: Chronic tendinopathies — Achilles, patellar, supraspinatus tendon
blog.70: Chronic neck and low back pain — with a significant muscular component
blog.71: Shoulder impingement syndrome — lateral and medial epicondylitis
blog.74: What to expect from a session?
blog.75: Before any procedure, the physiotherapist carries out a complete clinical assessment to identify the target tissues and plan the intervention. A dry needling session typically lasts 20 to 40 minutes, depending on the area treated and the number of points.
blog.76: Most patients describe the sensation during the technique as a deep pressure or heaviness, and occasionally a local cramping sensation when the trigger point is activated. This is precisely the sign that we are in the right place.
blog.77: After the session, it is normal to feel some local tenderness for 24 to 48 hours. Clinical improvement is often seen as early as the first or second session.
blog.79: It depends on the condition, its chronicity and the individual response. Acute conditions may resolve in 3 to 5 sessions. Chronic and complex conditions typically require 6 to 10 sessions, often combined with other treatments to maximise the result.
blog.80: Want to know if percutaneous physiotherapy is right for you?
blog.81: Book an assessment appointment with Dr. Cláudia Alves. We analyse your case and recommend the most suitable treatment.
blog.85: 5 habits that are destroying your posture (and how to fix them)
blog.86: We live in an age of increasingly compromised posture. Sedentary work, the phone and comfortable sofas are creating a generation with serious postural problems. But the good news is that most of it is reversible.
blog.88: On average, we look at our phones for more than 4 hours a day, with the neck tilted forward. Each degree of tilt multiplies the effective weight of the head on the cervical spine. At 60 degrees of tilt — a common position when using the phone — the head exerts about 27 kg of pressure on the cervical spine.
blog.89: Solution: Raise the phone to eye level, take frequent breaks and strengthen the posterior neck muscles with specific exercises.
blog.91: Spending hours in front of the computer with the screen too low, shoulders rolled forward and chin jutting out is the perfect recipe for neck pain, trapezius tension and headaches. The "forward head" position — head advanced relative to the centre of gravity — is now epidemic.
blog.92: Solution: Correct workstation ergonomics, screen at eye level, elbow support and 5-minute breaks every hour for mobilisation.
blog.94: This seemingly innocent habit creates an asymmetrical pelvic rotation that, over time, builds muscular imbalances between the left and right sides of the body. It is associated with functional scoliosis, low back pain and sacroiliac joint dysfunctions.
blog.95: Solution: Sit with your feet flat on the floor, parallel, with hips and knees at 90 degrees.
blog.97: Working or watching television on the sofa, especially in "curled up" positions, creates patterns of prolonged spinal flexion that overload the intervertebral discs and shorten the posterior muscle chain.
blog.98: Solution: Reserve the sofa for rest, not for work. If unavoidable, use a lumbar cushion and take frequent breaks.
blog.100: Posture is supported by muscles. Without adequate strengthening, especially of the deep core, the scapular stabilisers and the glutes, it is impossible to maintain correct posture throughout a full day. Muscle fatigue creates the same harmful postural patterns as the other habits.
blog.101: Solution: A regular exercise programme focused on postural strengthening. Clinical Pilates is particularly effective for this goal.
blog.103: Dr. Cláudia Alves carries out complete postural assessments and defines a personalised treatment plan.
blog.107: Baby colic: when paediatric osteopathy can help
blog.108: Colic affects up to one in four babies and is one of the main causes of stress in the first months of life. Inês Macedo explains the role of paediatric osteopathy in this context.
blog.110: Infant colic is defined as episodes of intense crying, of seemingly inexplicable cause, for at least 3 hours a day, 3 days a week, in babies under 3 months. It affects between 10 and 40% of babies, regardless of gender, feeding or type of birth.
blog.111: The exact cause remains debated. Theories include immaturity of the gastrointestinal system, an unbalanced gut microbiome, neurological hypersensitivity and, increasingly studied, dysfunctions of the autonomic nervous system related to the birth process.
blog.112: The role of birth in colic
blog.113: Birth, even when it goes well, is a physically demanding process for the baby. The compression and rotation forces during the birth canal, the use of a vacuum or forceps, or an emergency caesarean can create tension in the cranial tissues and cervical spine of the newborn.
blog.114: These tensions can affect the vagus nerve — the main nerve of the parasympathetic system that regulates digestion — and create patterns of hypersensitivity and regulation difficulties that manifest as colic, irritability and sleep difficulties.
blog.116: Paediatric osteopathy intervenes gently on these tensions, using techniques specific to babies that are completely painless and very gentle. The pressure applied is comparable to the weight of a sheet of paper.
blog.117: Cranial and cervical assessment to identify areas of tension
blog.118: Craniosacral techniques to release cranial compressions from birth
blog.119: Gentle mobilisations of the cervical spine and trunk
blog.120: Gentle visceral techniques to improve intestinal motility
blog.122: Most babies benefit from 2 to 4 sessions. Many parents report significant improvements as early as the first or second session — a calmer baby, better sleep quality and fewer crying episodes.
blog.123: Does your baby have intense colic?
blog.124: Inês Macedo offers paediatric osteopathy consultations for babies from their first days of life.
blog.128: Clinical Pilates vs. Fitness Pilates: what's the real difference?
blog.129: Clinical Pilates and fitness Pilates share some exercises, but they are completely different worlds. José Pereira explains what sets each approach apart and who Clinical Pilates is for.
blog.130: Fitness Pilates: for those who want shape and conditioning
blog.131: Fitness Pilates is an excellent exercise method for healthy people who want to improve their physical condition, flexibility and body awareness. Group classes follow general protocols, with progressive exercises suitable for most people.
blog.132: It is a valid method with proven benefits — but it is not therapeutic in itself. A fitness instructor is not a health professional, and a group class cannot be personalised for a specific injury.
blog.134: Clinical Pilates is prescribed and supervised by health professionals (physiotherapists or specialised therapists) as part of a clinical treatment plan. The differences are fundamental:
blog.135: Prior assessment: Before any session, there is a complete assessment — postural, functional and clinical
blog.136: Full personalisation: Each exercise has a specific therapeutic goal for that patient
blog.137: Constant supervision: The professional is present for every movement, correcting and adapting in real time
blog.138: Integration into treatment: Clinical Pilates is coordinated with physiotherapy, osteopathy and other treatments
blog.139: Controlled progression: Progress is monitored and adjusted to the patient's clinical response
blog.141: Clinical Pilates is indicated for people with:
blog.142: Chronic spinal pain — low back, neck and mid-back pain
blog.143: Post-orthopaedic surgery — knee, shoulder, hip, spine
blog.146: Sports injuries in the rehabilitation phase
blog.147: Active seniors who want to maintain mobility and prevent falls
blog.148: Want to start a Clinical Pilates programme?

blog.153: Low back pain: the back pain that affects 80% of Portuguese people
blog.154: Low back pain is the leading cause of work absenteeism in Portugal and one of the most treated conditions at Fisio Vitae. Dr. Cláudia Alves explains causes, risk factors and how physiotherapy can help.
blog.156: It is estimated that around 80% of the Portuguese population experiences at least one episode of low back pain during their lifetime. It is the leading cause of work incapacity and represents an enormous cost to the health system and the economy.
blog.157: What many people do not know is that, in most cases, low back pain is treatable and preventable. Physiotherapy plays a central role in treating this condition, with robust scientific evidence supporting its effectiveness.
blog.159: Sedentary lifestyle: Lack of movement weakens the muscles that support the spine
blog.160: Prolonged poor posture: Especially in an office-work context
blog.161: Mechanical overload: Improper lifting of weights, repetitive movements
blog.162: Disc herniation: Nerve-root compression that causes radiating pain (sciatica)
blog.163: Lumbar osteoarthritis: Wear of the intervertebral joints with age
blog.164: Psychosocial factors: Stress, anxiety and depression amplify the perception of pain
blog.166: You should seek a physiotherapist's assessment if the pain lasts more than 2 weeks without improvement, if it radiates to the lower limbs, if it is accompanied by tingling or muscle weakness, or if it significantly interferes with daily activities.
blog.167: There are also warning signs (red flags) that require urgent medical assessment: fever associated with the pain, unexplained weight loss, urinary incontinence or retention. These signs are rare but important.
blog.168: How does physiotherapy treat low back pain?
blog.169: The physiotherapeutic treatment of low back pain is multifactorial and personalised. At Fisio Vitae, we use a combination of manual techniques, therapeutic exercise and, when indicated, percutaneous physiotherapy for persistent muscle pain.

blog.175: The 6 stretches you should do every day (and why)
blog.176: Six minutes a day can make a huge difference. José Pereira presents the 6 essential stretches recommended by the Fisio Vitae team to maintain mobility and prevent pain.
blog.178: A sedentary lifestyle and working posture progressively shorten the muscles. Shortened muscles pull the joints into unfavourable positions, create tension, reduce range of motion and increase the risk of injury. Daily stretches counter this process simply and effectively.
blog.180: Lateral neck flexion: Tilt your head to the side, ear toward the shoulder. Hold for 30 sec, repeat on both sides. Relieves tension in the upper trapezius.
blog.181: Chest and shoulder stretch: With your arms behind your back, interlace your fingers and raise your arms slightly. Opens the chest and corrects rounded-shoulder posture.
blog.182: Psoas (hip flexor): In a half-kneeling position, gently push your hip forward. Essential for those who work seated — the psoas shortens with sitting time.
blog.183: Hamstrings: Lying on your back, bring one knee to your chest with your hands. Fundamental to protecting the lower back.
blog.184: Piriformis (glute): Lying down, cross one ankle over the opposite knee and gently pull the leg toward your chest. Releases tension in the sacral area and the sciatic nerve.
blog.185: Thorax (rotation): Sitting in a chair, rotate your trunk to each side, resting your hands on the backrest. Maintains thoracic mobility, which is often neglected.
blog.186: Each stretch should be held for 30 to 45 seconds, without pain, with calm breathing. Never force it — the stretch should be comfortable.

blog.192: Osteopathy: more than cracking backs — a holistic approach to health
blog.193: Osteopathy is often reduced to "cracking backs" in the popular imagination. Inês Macedo demystifies this discipline and explains what structural osteopathy really is.
blog.195: Osteopathy is not just back manipulation. It is not an alternative to conventional medicine. And it is not something mystical or without a scientific basis. Osteopathy is a recognised health profession, with extensive training (minimum 5 years) and grounding in anatomy, physiology and biomechanics.
blog.196: The fundamental principle: the body as a unit
blog.197: The central principle of osteopathy is that the human body functions as an integrated system. When there is dysfunction in one structure — whether articular, muscular, visceral or fascial — other structures are inevitably affected. The osteopath treats the system, not the isolated symptom.
blog.198: This explains why an osteopath may treat a headache by working on the neck and shoulders, treat reflux by working on the diaphragm, or treat low back pain by working on the feet. The logic is anatomical and physiological.
blog.199: What happens in an osteopathy consultation?
blog.200: The first consultation always begins with a detailed case history — clinical history, complaints, habits, previous surgeries and traumas. This is followed by a postural assessment and joint mobility tests. The treatment is then planned based on that assessment.
blog.201: The techniques used vary according to the case — they may include high-velocity manipulations (HVLA), muscle energy techniques, gentle mobilisations, fascial techniques or craniosacral techniques. The patient is always informed about what is going to happen.
blog.203: Inês Macedo offers complete osteopathic assessment consultations.
blog.207: Why the "core" is not just the abs (and what you should work on)
blog.208: The concept of "core" has become ubiquitous in fitness. But most people work only the superficial abdominals — and that can even worsen spinal problems.
blog.210: The core is the set of muscles that stabilise and control the centre of the body — the trunk. It includes far more than the famous abs (rectus abdominis) we see in advertising. The true core is made up of:
blog.211: Transversus abdominis: The deepest abdominal muscle, the true "belt" of the spine
blog.212: Multifidus: Deep spinal muscles that stabilise vertebra by vertebra
blog.213: Pelvic floor: The "base" of the trunk's stabilisation system
blog.214: Diaphragm: It's not just breathing — it's the "roof" of the core and essential for intra-abdominal pressure
blog.215: Glutes and hip rotators: They transfer forces between the trunk and the lower limbs
blog.216: The problem with classic abs exercises
blog.217: Classic abdominal exercises — crunches, sit-ups — predominantly work the rectus abdominis, which is a superficial "motor" muscle (it creates movement), not a stabiliser. Worse still, these exercises increase pressure on the lumbar intervertebral discs and can aggravate disc herniations and low back pain.
blog.219: Clinical Pilates focuses on activating the deep core muscles — especially the transversus abdominis and the multifidus — through controlled movements that respect the biomechanics of the spine. The result is a genuinely stable spine, not just visible abdominal muscles.
